methodology: Two kinds of number appear. SOURCED figures are attributed to a named source and date. INDICATIVE ranges are synthesised from recruiter salary guides (Hays, Carrington West, Penguin, Oyster), advertised vacancies and job-board data for 2025-26 - a considered market view, not a measurement. There is no central published database of UK planning salaries by grade, region, sector and specialism, so precise figures for every combination cannot be evidenced. Use this as a compass, not a contract: all figures are gross annual salary in pounds unless stated, and should be checked against the live recruiter guides, RTPI research and ONS before being relied upon.
interimBasis: Anchored to Carrington West advertised interim rates (2026, GBP35-42/hour for senior/principal development management); wider bands indicative. Rates vary with IR35 status, region and urgency.

regions.name: London
regions.comment: Highest nominal pay; highest living costs; London weighting in LA roles

regions.name: South East
regions.comment: Strong private market; commuter-belt pressure on pay

regions.name: East of England
regions.comment: Growth areas (Cambridge, corridors) pull pay up

regions.name: South West
regions.comment: Mixed; strong in Bristol, softer rurally

regions.name: North East
regions.comment: Lowest nominal English band; lower living costs

regions.name: Northern Ireland
regions.comment: Lower nominal pay; lower living costs; own system

regionBasis: Indicative index synthesised from recruiter guides and the direction of ONS regional earnings, 2025-26. UK = 100. Not a precise measurement; cost-of-living differences offset much of the nominal variation.

specialisms.why: Scarce expertise, major schemes, high fees and high commercial value
Core fee-earning work; steady demand and commercial exposure
Valued and often specialist, but rarely the top-paying niches
Essential public-interest work; pay bound by public scales and generalist supply

chartership.note: Direct uplift on qualifying is modest (indicatively GBP3,000-8,000). The real value is the GATEWAY effect: chartership (MRTPI) is in practice required for most senior and principal roles in both sectors, so it unlocks the grades where pay accelerates.

stages.comment: Uplift on award; eligible for senior roles

benefits.private: ~5-20%+; profit share / equity at senior level
At associate/director level in some firms

employers.pay: High base + bonus; strong at senior levels
employers.career: Commercial, land-focused; fast progression for deliverers
Broad role; strong local market knowledge

employers.pay: Mid-high base; bonus; equity at director

employers.career: Planning within a wider surveying business

sources.name: Hays — UK Salary & Recruiting Trends Guide 2026
sources.detail: Recruiter benchmark bands by grade and sector.

sources.name: Carrington West — 2026 Town Planning Salary Survey & Guide
sources.detail: Grade bands, public/private and interim rates; live vacancy rates cited for contractors.

sources.name: RTPI — State of the Profession 2025
sources.detail: Workforce, diversity and the long-run direction of pay (real median decline over 15 years).

sources.name: RTPI — Chartership (MRTPI) and APC guidance
sources.detail: The qualification underpinning the gateway effect.

sources.name: ONS — Annual Survey of Hours and Earnings (ASHE)
sources.detail: Occupation 'town planning officers' (SOC 2432) and regional earnings; nationally representative, broad occupation only.

sources.name: MHCLG — NPPF (Dec 2024) & Planning and Infrastructure Act 2025
sources.detail: Reform and housing-delivery context driving demand.

sources.name: Self-reported platforms (cross-check only)
sources.detail: PayScale (UK town planner average ~GBP31,000, 2026), Glassdoor, Indeed, Reed, Check-a-Salary. Junior-weighted and inconsistent; use as a sense-check only.
